evals.name: mythbusting-llms-txt-and-chunking
evals.prompt: I run a small Next.js site for my logistics company. Our SEO consultant just sent us a $4,500 proposal to make us 'AI search ready' and the main deliverables are: (1) publishing an /llms.txt file at our root that summarizes our services for LLMs, (2) restructuring all our service pages into 'AI-friendly chunks' (one Q&A per URL, so /road-freight/what-is-it, /road-freight/how-much, /road-freight/transit-time as separate pages), and (3) rewriting all our copy in a special 'extraction-friendly' style with declarative one-sentence paragraphs. He says without this we won't appear in Google's AI Overviews or ChatGPT. Should I accept the proposal?
evals.expected_output: Recommendation should clearly reject the proposal as framed. Should explain that Google does not use llms.txt for ranking/AI Overviews, that chunking content into one Q&A per URL is scaled content abuse that violates spam policy, and that AI-style rewriting is unnecessary because AI systems understand synonyms and meaning. Should redirect to what actually works (non-commodity content, technical foundations, business listings). Bonus: should note the one true nuance — llms.txt has a separate, optional role for AI agents browsing the site (Chrome's Lighthouse agentic-browsing check), but that is not ranking and does not justify the proposal's premise.

evals.name: non-commodity-content-service-page
evals.prompt:
Here's a draft of our road freight service page. It's not getting any organic traffic and I'm worried it won't show up in AI Overviews either when people search for road freight services. Can you tell me what's wrong and how to fix it?

---

ROAD FREIGHT SERVICES

Reliable Road Freight Solutions

At our company, we provide reliable and efficient road freight services across Europe. Our experienced team is dedicated to delivering your cargo safely and on time.

Why Choose Our Road Freight?

- Fast and reliable delivery
- Experienced drivers
- Modern fleet
- Competitive pricing
- 24/7 customer support
- Door-to-door service
- Tracking available

Our Coverage

We cover all major routes in Europe, including Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands, and more. Whether you need to ship small parcels or full truckloads, we have a solution for you.

Get a Quote Today

Contact us today to get a quote for your road freight needs. Our team is ready to help you with all your shipping requirements.

---

What should I do?
evals.expected_output: Diagnosis should identify this as commodity content — interchangeable with every competitor's page, no specific operational details, no first-hand expertise, no numbers, no real photos referenced. Recommendation should provide concrete examples of non-commodity content for a logistics service page (specific routes with transit times, actual fleet specifics, decision criteria like when to use road vs sea, real case examples) and explain why this matters for both classical and AI search. Bonus: tie to E-E-A-T with trust as the core component, and the 'remove the author, would anything remain' test.

evals.name: technical-foundations-nextjs-app
evals.prompt: I'm starting a new Next.js 14 project (app router) for a B2B SaaS marketing site — about 30 pages including a blog. What's the minimum technical SEO setup I need to make sure our pages are eligible to appear in Google AI Overviews and AI Mode, not just classical search? I'd like a concrete checklist I can implement, not vague advice.
evals.expected_output: Should provide a concrete Next.js-specific checklist covering: server-rendering primary content (avoid client-only critical content), generateMetadata with title/description/canonical/OG, app/sitemap.ts and app/robots.ts, semantic HTML structure, Core Web Vitals readiness, real anchor tags via next/link, image optimization with next/image, and Organization/BreadcrumbList JSON-LD. Should emphasize that AI eligibility = standard indexing eligibility (no separate AI optimization layer needed), anchor to Google's three minimum requirements (Googlebot not blocked, HTTP 200, indexable content), and note removed pages use noindex (kept crawlable) rather than a robots.txt block.

evals.name: two-surface-model-llms-txt-disambiguation
evals.prompt: Straight answer please: is llms.txt worth doing in 2026 or not? I keep getting contradictory advice. One blog says Google ignores it completely, another says Chrome's own Lighthouse now checks for it. Which is right?
evals.expected_output: Should resolve the apparent contradiction by separating the two surfaces rather than picking a side. For Google Search ranking / AI Overviews / AI Mode: llms.txt is not used and will not help you rank or get cited (cite Google's AI optimization guide position). For third-party AI agents browsing your site: Chrome's Lighthouse agentic-browsing category does check for an optional llms.txt as a discoverability hint (N/A if absent, not a failure). Conclusion should be that both blogs are partially right because they're talking about different surfaces; llms.txt is optional and only relevant to agent navigability, never to ranking. Should not overstate its value.

evals.name: agent-ready-website-webmcp
evals.prompt: We're a flight-booking startup. Our investors keep saying we need to be 'AI agent ready' so that assistants like Gemini or ChatGPT can book through us. What does that actually mean technically, and what should we do first? Don't give me marketing fluff — I want to know the real standards and where to start.
evals.expected_output: Should route this to Surface B (agent usability), explicitly distinguishing it from SEO/ranking. Should explain that agents read the DOM and the accessibility tree (their primary data model) and use visual/coordinate interaction, so the foundation is a serious accessibility pass: semantic elements, accessible names/labels, valid a11y tree, stable layout (low CLS), no ghost overlays, real labeled forms, resumable URLs. Should introduce WebMCP as the emerging standard for declaring tools to agents (declarative form annotations toolname/tooldescription/toolparamdescription as the low-risk starting point; imperative navigator.modelContext.registerTool for richer flows) and note it's a proposed standard / Chrome origin trial. Should mention the Lighthouse agentic-browsing category as the measurement (pass-ratio, not 0-100). For checkout specifically, should mention UCP (Google+Shopify, powers agentic checkout in AI Mode/Gemini) but with a 'keep a clean agent-operable web checkout, watch the spec, don't hand-roll it yet' stance. Should start with accessibility + key-form WebMCP annotations, not a rewrite.

evals.name: ai-generated-content-and-crawler-controls
evals.prompt: Two questions. First, we're using AI to write a lot of our product descriptions and generate product images for our store — is that going to get us penalized by Google? Second, our legal team wants to block AI crawlers via robots.txt; will adding Google-Extended hurt our search rankings?
evals.expected_output: On AI content: should explain it's not banned but must meet Search Essentials and spam policies; mass-producing pages without added value risks the scaled content abuse policy. Crucially for ecommerce, should surface the labeling requirements: AI-generated images need IPTC DigitalSourceType 'TrainedAlgorithmicMedia' metadata, and AI-generated product titles/descriptions must be labeled as AI-generated (Merchant Center). On crawler controls: should reassure that Google-Extended is a robots.txt token controlling Gemini training/grounding use only, and that it 'does not impact a site's inclusion in Google Search nor is it used as a ranking signal' — so blocking it is a content-licensing/policy decision, orthogonal to SEO, and will not hurt rankings.
